data.title: The Dividend Aristocrats Ranked By Quality Scores (April 2026)
data.description:
The S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Index contains 69 dividend growth [DG] stocks. Compiled by S&P Dow Jones Indices, the index contains S&P 500 companies that have increased their annual dividends for 25 or more years.

In this article, I rank DG stocks by 9F Quality Scores, my new quality scoring system that rates dividend stocks on a 10-point scale across nine weighted factors. Each factor blends qualitative expert assessments from independent sources with quantitative financial metrics, such as payout ratios, growth rates, and leverage.

I present undervalued, fairly valued, and overvalued Dividend Aristocrats, identify candidates for further research and possible inclusion in your portfolio, and provide a downloadable spreadsheet containing fundamental and added-value data for the Dividend Aristocrats.

Previously, I used the average dividend yield method and several fair value estimates and price targets to estimate a stock's fair value [FV]. My expanded valuation system uses the same wisdom-of-the-crowds philosophy, but adds calculated estimates and rigorous statistical analysis to arrive at a consensus FV.

This article updates one I published in March 2025 and uses a similar layout and descriptions.

About the Dividend Aristocrats

The S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats Index measures the equal-weighted performance of companies within the S&P 500 that have followed a managed-dividends policy of consistently increasing dividends for at least 25 consecutive years.

For details on eligibility for inclusion, diversification criteria, and special rules for spin-offs, mergers, and acquisitions, please refer to the S&P Dividend Aristocrats Indices Methodology.

On January 23, 2026, S&P Dow Jones Indices announced the annual rebalancing results for the S&P 500 Dividend Aristocrats, indicating that no membership changes will be made before the opening of trading on February 2, 2026.

As a result, the index remains at 69 constituents.

Quality Assessment

In my previous articles ranking dividend growth stocks, I relied on a system modeled after David Van Knapp's Quality Snapshots. While the system served me well for many years, it does not handle missing data gracefully, nor does it integrate any quantitative metrics.

This situation led me to develop 9F Quality Scores, a new quality scoring system on a 10-point scale that bridges the gap between expert qualitative measures and derived quantitative metrics across nine distinct factors:

  1. Dividend Safety

  2. Dividend Track Record

  3. Financial Strength

  4. Earnings & Revenue Growth

  5. Profitability & Earnings Quality

  6. Risk-Adjusted Returns

  7. Credit Rating & Capital Structure

  8. Moat & Competitive Advantage

  9. Governance & ESG

I don't weigh these factors equally. Dividend Safety is the heavyweight: a dividend cut destroys both income and principal, and often the ability to recover quickly. Earnings & Revenue Growth follow closely behind, as they form the engine that makes dividend growth possible. Profitability & Earnings Quality, Financial Strength, and Dividend Track Record round out the top-tier factors. They measure whether the business model actually delivers for shareholders. The remaining factors carry lower weights but add good context.

To build factor scores, I aggregate data from nine sources, including Value Line, Morningstar, Simply Safe Dividends, and Seeking Alpha. Using multiple sources helps me filter out the noise of any single analyst's bias. When a specific metric is missing, I don't just give up on the stock. Rather, I adjust the score using the available data and apply a small penalty to account for the unknown. This ensures the final composite score is both fair and robust.

Here is a table mapping 9F Quality Scores to quality labels and investment grades:

Stock Valuation

Determining fair value shouldn't be a guessing game based on a single model. That's why I use a Wisdom-of-the-Crowds approach that pulls in up to 40 independent data points or opinions per stock.

Twenty of these opinions come from industry heavyweights like Finbox, GuruFocus, and FASTGraphs. The other twenty are my own calculations, based on mean-reversion methods such as Dividend Yield Theory and P/E reversion.

But here is the secret sauce: I don't implicitly trust any of the opinions. Each opinion passes through a two-stage Bayesian shrinkage process. Think of it as a reliability filter. If a source has a spotty track record or the stock itself is notoriously unpredictable, the model pulls that opinion back towards the current market price.

Abbott (ABT) and Roper Technologies (ROP) are good examples. Both have 24 opinions, but they tell very different stories. ABT's opinions are tightly clustered, giving us a high-confidence Buy signal with modest upside. ROP, meanwhile, has opinions scattered all over the map. Even though it shows a higher upside, the low confidence score warns us that the underlying data is noisy.

Key Metrics and Valuation

Each table below presents key metrics, my consensus FV, and quality scores:

  • Yrs: years of consecutive dividend increases

  • Fwd Yld: forward dividend yield for a recent share Price

  • 5Y Avg Yld: 5-year average dividend yield

  • 5Y DGR: 5-year compound annual growth rate of the dividend

  • 5Y EGR: 5-year compound annual growth rate of earnings per share

  • 5Y RGR: 5-year compound annual growth rate of revenue

  • 5Y Tot Ret: 5-year compound trailing total returns

  • Proj DGR: projected dividend growth rate

  • 5Y YoC: projected yield on cost after five years of investment

  • ACN: Adjusted Chowder Number (see below)

  • Price: recent share price

  • FV: Consensus fair value

  • FV Upside: Upside from Price to FV

  • Qual: 9F Quality Score out of 10.0

Color-coding

  • Ticker: based on quality label

  • ACN: colored based on likelihood of delivering annualized returns of 8%

  • Price: green if Price < FV

I color-code the ACN column in my spreadsheets as follows:

  • Green (ACN ≥ 20) - very likely to return at least 8% annually

  • Yellow (12 ≤ ACN < 20) - likely to return at least 8% annually

  • Red (ACN < 12) - less likely to return at least 8% annually

I own five of the 41 fairly-valued Dividend Aristocrats: ATO, CTAS, CVX, LOW, and MCD.

ATO (+23 shares), CVX (+170 shares), LOW (+31 shares), and MCD (+8 shares) are overweight in my portfolio.

CVX has experienced a significant run-up this year, rising about 37% in 2026 to an all-time high of $214.71 before pulling back to current levels. The surge stems from a sharp rise in energy prices due to the war with Iran and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz. But CVX has also capitalized on several growth opportunities, bolstering investor confidence.

Given how overweight my CVX position is, I'm planning to trim my position (at least partially) to get closer to my target weight.

I'll wait for better opportunities to trim ATO, LOW, and MCD.

My CTAS position is underweight by 30 shares. I'm planning on making it a full-sized position, as it's one of only 10 stocks rated Exceptional based on 9F Quality Scores.

None of the fairly valued Dividend Aristocrats interests me at this time.

Charts

The following is a visual representation of the Dividend Aristocrats' quality scores in rank order from lowest to highest:

The chart highlights the eight Dividend Aristocrats I hold in my portfolio.

And, here is a ranking chart showing the discount to my fair value estimates of the Dividend Aristocrats:

Top Opportunities

Let's now use some screens to find Dividend Aristocrats with compelling metrics.

Projected Dividend Growth Rate

Companies pay dividends from earnings, and earnings depend on revenue. The projected dividend growth rate [Proj DGR] is a conservative estimate of future dividend growth based on a stock's historical 5-year dividend, earnings, and revenue growth rates.

I ignore the maximum of the three 5-year growth rates and average the lower pair, further limiting the Proj DGR to 30%:

Proj DGR = min(30%,average(min(D, E, R), median(D, E, R)))

where:

  • D = 5-year dividend growth rate

  • E = 5-year earnings growth rate

  • R = 5-year revenue growth rate

BRO and CTAS have double-digit percentage Proj DGRs.

BRO tops the list with a Proj DGR of 14.4%. The stock is undervalued, with an FV upside of 9.4%, and would be a good choice for growth-oriented investors under $73 per share. BRO's quality score is 7.7.

CTAS is fairly valued and trades just above my consensus FV of $167.75. In my view, CTAS is the better choice for growth-oriented investors. As mentioned earlier, I own CTAS and rate it Exceptional based on its 9.0 quality score.

Adjusted Chowder Number

I consider the Adjusted Chowder Number [ACN] to determine a stock's total return prospects. In calculating the ACN, I use the projected dividend growth rate instead of the 5-year dividend growth rate:

ACN = min(10%,Y) + Proj DGR

where:

Y = forward dividend yield

I cap the forward dividend yield at 10% because I'm suspicious of yields above 10%.

ADP, BRO, CTAS, and NEE have ACNs above 12.

ADP and BRO are undervalued and good candidates for investors seeking strong total return prospects.

CTAS and NEE are fairly valued and would be suitable candidates below $167 (CTAS) and $93 (NEE).

Projected 5-Year Yield on Cost

The projected 5-year yield on cost [5Y YoC] is an income-oriented metric that indicates the expected yield on cost after buying and holding a stock for 5 years.

To calculate the 5Y YoC, I use the stock's forward dividend yield and projected dividend growth rate:

5Y YoC = min(10%,Y) × (1 + Proj DGR)^5

where:

Y = forward dividend yield

Here, ^5 means to the 5th power.

As mentioned earlier, I cap the forward dividend yield at 10%.

The following stocks have 5Y YoC's above 5%: ADP, AMCR, ES, ESS, FRT, GPC, HRL, O, SJM, and TROW.

ADP, HRL, and SJM are undervalued. With its yield of 3.48% and a quality score of 7.6, ADP is a great candidate for income-oriented investors. While SJM offers a higher yield, its quality score is only 4.7, which I rate Below Average.

AMCR, ES, ESS, FRT, GPC, O, and TROW are fairly valued.

TROW has a quality score of 6.1, rated Above Average. It offers an attractive 5.4% yield.

ESS yields 4.14% and O yields 5.1%. Both have quality scores rated Average.

I'm not interested in AMCR, ES, FRT, and GPC because they're all rated Below Average.

5-year Trailing Total Return

The 5-year trailing total return [5Y Tot Ret] metric includes returns from stock price appreciation (or depreciation) and dividend payments over the trailing 5-year period.

I like to see a 5Y Tot Ret of at least 25%. Below, I've charted 5-TTRs of Dividend Aristocrats above 10%:

The top-performing Dividend Aristocrats over the trailing 5-year period are CAH, CAT, GWW, WMT, and XOM, all with 5Y Tot Ret's above 25%.

CAT, GWW, and WMT are overvalued.

CAH and XOM are fairly valued. With its quality score of 7.1 (High Quality), CAH is the strongest candidate based on past performance and current valuation, but only below $210 per share. XOM's quality score is 6.8 (Above Average), and the stock is a buy below $146 per share.

Concluding Remarks

In this article, I ranked the 69 Dividend Aristocrats by quality score and presented them in separate tables according to valuation: undervalued, overvalued, and fairly valued.

My new quality scoring system blends qualitative and quantitative metrics and rates dividend stocks on a 10-point scale across nine weighted factors.

I expanded my valuation system by adding more crowdsourced fair values and price target opinions. Additionally, I added calculated estimates and rigorous statistical analysis to determine a consensus FV.

Of the 69 Dividend Aristocrats covered in this article:

  • 48 have Investment Grade ratings based on their quality scores ≥ 6.0.

  • 16 stocks are undervalued based on FV upside ≥ 5%

  • 12 stocks are overvalued based on FV upside ≤ -5%

  • 27 stocks have quality scores ≥ 7.0, making them genuinely high-quality stocks!

Here are some candidates to consider depending on your investment style:

  • For growth: CTAS (below $167) and BRO

  • For total return: CTAS (below $167), ADP, and BRO

  • For income: ADP, HRL, TROW, ESS, and O

  • Based on past performance: CAH (below $210) and XOM (below $146)

As always, I encourage you to do your due diligence before investing!

I'm providing a downloadable spreadsheet of the Dividend Aristocrats. The spreadsheet includes the data presented in this article. I hope this snapshot of fundamental and added value proves helpful in analyzing the Dividend Aristocrats.

Note that I cannot grant permission to edit the spreadsheet, as it is a read-only file that every Seeking Alpha reader can access!

But you can create a copy of the spreadsheet to edit. Click on this link to the Dividend Aristocrats spreadsheet.

To copy the spreadsheet, sign in with a Google account and select File | Make a Copy to save a copy of the spreadsheet on your computer.

Thanks for reading, and happy investing!

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: USA Compression Partners: For The Covered Yield And Moderate Upside
data.description:
USA Compression Partners (USAC) offers contract compression services for the natural gas infrastructure. Compression is necessary for moving gas through pipelines and processing. Its revenue comes from fixed-fee contracts tied to compression capacity, not volumes — so USAC is largely free from commodity exposure. In January 2026, the company acquired J-W Power for ~$860M, adding over 800k horsepower and introducing in-house manufacturing capability.

USAC had $613.8 million in EBITDA and $385.7 million in distributable cash flow in 2025. Annual distribution was around $304 million. So they had ~1.25x coverage ratio, decent if not strong, leaving some excess cash after distributions.

At the current share price of around $26-27, forward distribution yield is ~7.8%. The reported ~250% payout ratio is based on GAAP earnings. But this is due to accounting effects, primarily depreciation and interest.

The big news is that USAC closed the ~$860M J-W Power acquisition on January 12, 2026. This introduces in-house compression manufacturing capability to USAC. Management now guides to $770–800 million in EBITDA and $480–510 million in DCF for 2026, which will improve coverage to ~1.6x.

With earnings happening on May 5, the key question is whether early post-acquisition performance supports management’s ~$500M DCF run-rate.

Thesis: I rate USAC a Buy as a dependable midstream-like business with a strong distribution adequately covered by income. I remain mildly cautious and will improve my rating if the coverage improves sustainably given leverage, ongoing capex, and if they properly execute the integration of J-W Power.

Business Model

USA Compression engages in natural gas compression, something that is required to move gas through pipelines at the right systemic pressure.

The company operates through contracts, typically fixed-fee and long-term, ranging from six months to five years. Typically, also, customers pay for compression capacity regardless of throughput. So the revenue profile is far more stable than upstream producers, the reason why I called it midstream-like earlier. The company has no direct exposure to commodity prices, and even the fuel required for compression is usually supplied by the customers. So it removes short-term sensitivity to oil and gas prices, even though sustained movement can create pressure.

USAC is a tightly run business with fleet utilization at above ~94%. Pricing power has improved, with average horsepower pricing reaching $21.69 per HP. The J-W acquisition increases fleet size and adds manufacturing capability, but its value depends on deploying ~200k idle horsepower rather than cost savings. In the upcoming earnings and going forward, I will be closely watching if they are able to utilize more of J-W’s undeployed capacity – a primary lever for future growth.

J-W Power Acquisition

The J-W acquisition increases USAC’s fleet and footprint. J-W brings over 800k active horsepower, so the combined fleet now has ~4.4M active HP.

Geographically, J-W operates in all major US production basins, including the Permian, Northeast (Marcellus/Utica), Rockies, Mid-Continent, and Gulf Coast. The acquisition now broadens USAC’s presence. The acquisition adds ~30+ locations, 300+ customers, and ~16+ years of average customer tenure.

The current USAC focus is on infrastructure-scale compression. J-W’s assets are focused on similar mid-to-large horsepower units, with ~70% of the fleet in the 400–2,000+ HP range.

Apart from contract compression, J-W also engages in aftermarket services and manufacturing capabilities, including parts distribution and fabrication. This business can be vertically integrated into USAC’s model. Currently it relies on third-party suppliers for some of these things.

J-W capacity was more underutilized than USAC’s, with around 81% utilization total. When properly integrated, this gives USAC room for growth through more utilization. This is important because growth and not cost synergies is what drove the deal. The company paid ~$860M to acquire J-W, and they said this amounts to $10–20 million of expected annual run rate synergies over time. This is just 1-2% of the deal value.

Now look at growth through utilizing the underutilized HP units. J-W has nearly 200k HP remaining undeployed. At the current pricing of roughly $21.69 per HP per month, each 10,000 HP deployed generates ~$2.6 million annual revenue. So, if 50,000 HP gets redeployed, that is $13M incremental annual revenue per year. That is 25% of J-W’s total current undeployed capacity. If the entire capacity can be deployed — given the situation in the Middle East, not an altogether implausible scenario — that is $52M in incremental revenue per year from the acquisition.

Even full deployment adds only a mid-single digit percentage to EBITDA, so this is incremental improvement. Even so, this is the primary metric to see if the acquisition has worked, so I will be watching for this.

Distribution Quality

In 2025, USAC generated $385.7M in DCF and paid out ~$304M in distributions, leaving ~$82M in retained cash. This is the buffer supporting the distribution. After the acquisition, management is guiding to $480–510M DCF. At the ~304M distribution level, the buffer will now be ~$176–206M in retained cash. Coverage will improve to ~1.58x–1.68x. This will give their balance sheet considerable flexibility.

Post-acquisition, USAC carries about $2.54B of debt. Against 2026 EBITDA guidance of $770–800M, that implies debt / EBITDA of roughly ~3.2x–3.3x. That is manageable, but still leaves the company exposed to utilization weakness. If EBITDA falls 10%, leverage would rise to roughly ~3.5x–3.6x, while interest expense remains largely fixed at ~$160–180M. That would reduce the cash available for growth capex and debt reduction.

Currently, interest takes out ~40-45% of 2025 DCF. Post acquisition, this goes down to ~30-35% of guided DCF – still substantial. Deleveraging should be a key concern for USAC.

Apart from leverage, other things that impact distribution safety are that they should maintain or improve utilization, and price should remain at current levels at the least.

This also explains why there has been little distribution growth — retained cash is getting absorbed by balance sheet needs. Management must prioritize either deleveraging or growth, as both cannot be adequately funded at the same time. If they focus on deleveraging, coverage will improve. Focusing immediately on additional growth right after a major acquisition may not improve distribution immediately. This will be something to watch for in the upcoming earnings.

Near-Term Catalyst: Upcoming Earnings

USAC is expected to report earnings on May 5, 2026. This is important as it would be the first clean look at post-acquisition performance. I would watch not just the EBITDA, but the underlying drivers.

First, utilization. USAC ended 2025 with a high ~94%, while J-W only did ~81%. I have said that growth will come from incremental utilization especially at J-W. So I would be very interested to see if there are signs of that.

Second is pricing. That is the second lever of growth, and current/2025 pricing was ~21.69 per HP. This needs to stay stable, increase if possible, so I would be watching for that.

The third lever is DCF conversion. Company guidance was $480-510M, so it is important that early tracking comes near the ~495M midpoint. If initial results come in below run-rate, coverage weakens.

Fourth is the debt situation. With ~2.5B debt, the key question is whether retained cash gets directed towards deleveraging or reinvestment. If leverage increases, that would be a negative signal.

This earnings release is less about reported numbers and more about confirming these few things — that the company is actively focusing on utilization and capital discipline and that the pricing is holding or improving.

Valuation

At current prices, USAC pays an annual distribution of $2.10, which implies roughly 7.8% forward yield. 145 million shares are outstanding, so the market cap is around $3.9B. DCF guidance is $480-510M for 2026. Using the midpoint of $495 million, this implies a DCF yield of ~12.7% on the current equity value.

Total debt is around $2.5B, so enterprise value is around $6.4B. Cash on hand is negligible. Using midpoint EBITDA guidance of ~$785M, the stock trades at roughly ~8.2x forward EV/EBITDA. For context, the midstream sector median forward multiple is approximately ~6.7x, while larger operators like Energy Transfer trade closer to ~8.6x forward EBITDA, or about ~29% above the sector median. To be noted, USAC has higher leverage compared to peers.

The ~5% gap between DCF yield and distribution yield translates to roughly ~$190M annually. This is the balance after interest and maintenance capex. This is the actual cash available for growth capex, deleveraging and so on. It is not entirely freely available to shareholders because the company must concentrate on deleveraging. To move leverage from ~3.2x toward a more conservative ~2.5–3.0x range, the company would need to allocate roughly ~$100–200M per year toward debt reduction. That means, most of this cash is effectively committed rather than freely available.

The upside case for USAC depends on whether it can sustain DCF at or above the current guidance range. If DCF can be stabilized around $500M, and the market prices the business at a 10% yield, we have an implied equity value of roughly $5B. That gives us $34-35 per share on the current outstanding of ~145M shares. That is a 27-31% upside from current levels. On the other hand, if DCF falls to around $400M, and the required yield moves to 12%, the implied equity value goes down to $3.3B, or about $23 per share. That is roughly ~14% downsizing from current levels.

Conclusion

USAC is a dependable midstream-like business with stable cash flows and improving coverage. The balance sheet, with ~$2.5B debt and ~3.2-3.3x leverage, is a constraint. That leverage makes most of the ~$190M retained cash committed to deleveraging.

At ~8.2x forward EBITDA and ~12–13% DCF yield, USAC is not cheap relative to the sector median. Upside of ~30% requires stable DCF and modest rerating. A downside of ~14% may happen if capacity remains underutilized. USAC is not a growth story

I rate USAC a cautious Buy, dependent on execution and balance sheet discipline.

data.title: PrimeEnergy Resources: Buy On The Adverse News
data.description:
The last article mentioned that PrimeEnergy Resources (PNRG) had no long-term debt. The current 10-K reports still show no long-term debt and some common share repurchases. The company is still heavily dependent upon the decisions of operating partners for a fair amount of its business. The biggest change since the last article is that oil prices dropped today in a big way. But a debt-free company with a largely unused bank line has the ability to finance whatever its partners want to do (whenever they want to do it). This is a company that simply "cashes checks" while waiting for a commodity price recovery.

What is actually interesting is that despite the news today, oil prices are still considerably above the levels of the previous fiscal year. That is likely to remain the case as the Iranian situation did do significant damage to the oil and gas industry in the area. It will take some time to repair the facilities and get production and processing back to the prewar levels.

More importantly, despite the opening of the Strait of Hormuz, anyone with a knowledge of the history of the area knows that issues in the Middle East are rarely resolved rapidly. The project that is going on is likely to turn out to be long-term rather than a quickly done deal as was the case in Venezuela.

If that is the case, then a debt-free company like PrimeEnergy Resources is in a very good position to take advantage of higher commodity prices. This is a company that has "name" operators. It therefore can likely increase production at will once the operators decide that the acreage should be developed. All that was really needed was higher oil prices. Now those higher oil prices exist. We will therefore soon find out what it takes for the acreage that the company has to experience the higher activity it saw back in fiscal year 2024.

Earnings

One of the axioms of nearly any commodity industry is that when commodity prices are low, earnings are low as well. That often makes for a relatively high price-earnings ratio. That high price-earnings ratio may make an investor think that the stock is expensive when the reality is that the earnings are at the low point in the business cycle.

Note that there are some instruments outstanding that provide for substantial dilution of the currently outstanding stock. However, even given that the earnings per share are more than reasonable considering that commodity prices dropped during the current fiscal year compared to the last fiscal year.

Even with the decline in commodity prices (emphasis on the liquid prices), the company still remained profitable. Note that the price of oil (for example) only had to drop about $10 per barrel (very roughly) to cut earnings in half.

But now, oil prices not only recovered to the levels seen in fiscal year 2024, but they also went way past that. If prices remain elevated in the WTI $80s range or higher, then fully diluted earnings could reasonably top $30 per share in the current fiscal year. Any production increases would be "icing on the cake" and make that figure even better.

To me, that makes this an extremely cheap idea for the current situation. Even though the market reacted harshly to the latest news, before that news we were all praying for oil prices like the current ones. Yet much of the industry is not reflecting those current prices.

I believe that much of the industry is not reflecting the higher prices because there has been no communication about the goals in the situation with Iran. That has led to uncertainty about the prospects of higher prices (and hence the lackluster industry really in response to those higher prices).

But now, what is likely to happen is damage reports, volumes out of production as a result, and timetables to get things back the way that they were. That should provide some certainty to an industry very wary of higher prices "dissolving tomorrow". As a result, the stock prices should go higher with that certainty.

If the hostilities resume, that would likely mean more production that is "out of order" for the time being. Therefore, I see very little chance of commodity price downside from current levels for the time being. But that has to go with the warnings (and risk) that commodity prices are very volatile as well as low future visibility. So, all kinds of things can happen as the future unfolds.

Summary

PrimeEnergy Resources is a company that does not do "roadshows" or really any form of promotion. As such, it will likely remain a bit cheaper than much of the industry that does quarterly earnings conference calls and "roadshows."

However, the debt-free balance sheet takes away a whole lot of upstream financial risks that exist with the rest of the industry. Even though the company does not control a whole lot of operating decisions, that debt free balance sheet speaks volumes about the profitability of the company. It also shows an ability to handle a wide variety of situations that may come with higher oil prices.

The recent decline in the stock price due to the opening of the Strait of Hormuz makes this a strong buying opportunity. I find it hard to believe there is much downside from current oil price levels for the reasons discussed above. Therefore, this company is likely to "print money" at current oil price levels.

I also believe that once damage reports "come in" from "everywhere" there will be some certainty to the situation that has not existed in the past. This will enable the industry to form a long-term strategy for the industry to navigate the situation. That should lead to better prices throughout the industry (including here).

But this company can also grow by acquisition (as it has occasionally in the past). It can also grow by increasing production as its partners chose to do back in fiscal year 2024. While the company cannot control where its partners choose to drill, if they chose company acreage once, they are likely to do it again with the return of higher commodity prices.

Risks

The company does not control the development of substantial amounts of its acreage. Therefore, it is at the mercy of the choices of its partners as to whether or not production will increase or decline.

So far, the company has had good luck in obtaining acreage that was developed for production. But there is no assurance that luck will continue in the future.

A debt-free balance sheet is a huge asset in the upstream industry. A material change leading to financial leverage would cause a review of the future prospects of the company.

data.title: Nu Holdings: Scale, Efficiency, And Earnings Still Support A Buy
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome GT Research as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Introduction

Nu Holdings Ltd. (NU) is the largest digital banking platform in Latin America and one of the largest worldwide. The company was founded in Brazil, is expanding across Latin America, and its price is down about 18% from its all-time high, two months ago. Besides expanding to other countries, the company has reached a level in scale and efficiency that is impressive. So is it a good time to buy the stock?

I have been a Nubank customer for years in Brazil, and I am particularly satisfied with all the digital banking services provided, and hopefully I can bring a different view of the company. My personal experience may help explain why the company has built such a strong presence in Brazil. Nu has been able to reach 62% of adult population in Brazil, scaling quickly. For those who have never heard of Nubank, think of it as SoFi Technologies, Inc. (SOFI) for Latin America. The company recently expanded to Mexico and Colombia, reaching 15% and 11% of the adult population.

Overview Of The Business

Nubank, the operating name and digital banking brand, has been showing growth in its customer base, with a CAGR of 22% over the last 5 years. Not only has the number of customers increased, but the activity ratio has too. Average revenue per active client has risen 30% per year over the last 5 years, meaning that Nubank is getting more clients, who are getting more active on the platform, and generating more revenue per client for the bank, which is a sustainable form of growth.

To me, the numbers below not only support all this growth, but also show that the business still has a lot of room to keep growing. Purchase volume grew 17%, and monthly average revenue per active customer increased 23%. I think this suggests not only that Nubank can offer different products to a different group of clients, but also that its customers are changing. Many customers that first joined were because they didn't have access to traditional banks, but Nubank is becoming a more respected bank, not just a fintech focused on subprime clients.

This shift in customer profile is worth examining more closely. In its early years, a significant portion of Nubank's Brazilian customer base were of underbanked or subprime borrowers, which was the strategy of the bank, serving a neglected segment to expand quickly. However, this meant carrying higher risks, which is visible in the historically elevated 90+ NPL ratios.

But Nubank has changed the composition of that base. As the company's brand matured, it began attracting customers with stronger credit profiles, such as salaried workers, small business owners, and higher-income individuals who preferred the digital experience. This change is reflected in the change of monthly average revenue per active customer that I mentioned before, which also reflects in the credit quality curve of the company. And after all this growth in the business, I believe that the company may not take the same risks it did in Brazil in the first few years of the company, which could result in a cleaner portfolio profile than Brazil.

What also caught my attention was total portfolio growth of 40% YoY, which shows the level of expansion the company has reached. Deposits also rose 29% YoY, which helps revenue not only directly, but also by lowering funding costs and giving the business more room to originate new loans. To be honest, I am really impressed by these results, especially because expansion across Mexico and Colombia just started, while the case in Brazil has already proved to be successful.

I think it's important to understand this growth in deposits, mostly how it's being played in each country. Brazil is Nu Holdings' biggest market, but the company is still being able to grow deposits there. The company is also growing deposits in Colombia, but there is still a lot of room for growth there. In Mexico, although deposits are lower than Q2'25, Q4'25 is 40% higher compared to Q4'24. But Nubank is still in the early stages of its expansion to Colombia and Mexico, so I see the company still having significant growth potential ahead.

The founders of Nubank, David Vélez and Cristina Junqueira, are still involved in the business, and I believe they are the right people to guide Nubank through its expansion to Colombia and Mexico. I think it's a great differentiator that, while Cristina is Brazilian, David is Colombian, and understanding the culture of those countries in this scenario is key.

To me, Nu is beating traditional banks because its model is much easier to scale. Traditional banks usually carry heavier branch networks, slower product cycles, and more operational complexity, while Nubank has been able to grow with a much lighter structure. A simpler platform makes it easier to launch products faster, reuse infrastructure, and deepen customer relationships through more cross-selling.

In my view, this is important because it means that the company can add new customers cheaply and they stay active, generating more revenue at a low cost over time. That's why I think that the model that worked in Brazil has a real chance to keep working in Mexico and Colombia and compete against big banks, just like it did in Brazil.

Financial Review

In my opinion, gross profit and net income CAGR show how much Nubank has matured over the years. They both keep growing strongly, net income has grown at a CAGR of 152% since 2022, and the company is still expanding. Nubank is growing, becoming more efficient, and more profitable.

Another number that caught my attention was the efficiency ratio. The ratio fell from 78% in 2021 to 19.9% in Q4'25, meaning that the cost relative to revenue is falling significantly, giving Nubank a competitive advantage for a company that still grows very fast. This shows that the company is scaling pretty well, while keeping ROE steady over the last 6 semesters, close to 30%.

Looking at the products Nubank offers, the company segments its gross profit into credit, fees, and float. Credit has been dominant over the last quarters, since it was the first product the bank offered, but the combination of fees and float has been growing meaningfully since Q3'24, showing that Nubank is becoming less dependent on credit performance alone. Credit it now responsible for 42% of gross profit, compared to 46% in Q4'24, while float grew from 18% to 29% in the same period, and fees kept stable, decreasing only 2%. I see this as a positive sign, since holding and growing deposits for example is becoming more profitable for the firm, making the business more resilient during credit stress periods.

Analyzing the company geographically, revenue is still very concentrated in Brazilian market. According to Factset, Brazil still accounts for roughly 91% of total revenue as of December 2025, while Mexico contributes with only 6.7%, and Colombia is even lower. This shows that Nubank still has a lot of room to grow its business In Mexico and Colombia, and this geographic concentration in Brazil should gradually decrease, which would reduce its country-specific risk.

Revenue and Normalized Diluted EPS have risen over the last few years, with revenue hitting double-digit growth since 2020. The company is expanding to Colombia and Mexico, and this year Nubank received approval to establish a US national bank and it plans to start operations in 2027.

Nubank's 15-90 NPLs have been at their lowest level for the last 3 years, which is a good sign of how the loan business is doing. Different from that, 90+ NPLs are high compared to prior quarters. The Brazilian economy has been facing a lot of problems, and a recession would not be a surprise. The country is the biggest market for Nubank, and has a current interest rate close to 15%.

Valuation

Nu Holdings looks attractive to me right now. The stock trades at $15.43, with a P/E multiple of about 26x. When we look at EPS estimates, we see projected growth of 45% in 2026 and 33% in 2027, with a forward P/E in 2027 of 13.9x. This P/E is close to what traditional banks trade at, but NU is still growing, showing better efficiency, and that is key for me. I don't think Nubank should trade close to a traditional bank.

Comparing Nubank with some peers that operate in the U.S., NU looks undervalued. SoFi, which could be used as a comparable company because of the services it provides, currently trades at a P/E of 48x. I understand Nubank trades at a discount compared to companies based in the U.S. because of all the risks, but it's almost half of SoFi and the risks Nu takes have proven to be an opportunity.

I made the table below assuming a bull, base, and bear case. For the last 2 years, Nubank has traded at an average P/E a little bit over 30x, so for the base case I will use 26x to be more conservative. For my bear case, I will use 12x, which usually what with traditional banks trade at, and for my bull case I will use 40x, which is lower than what SoFi and other digital banks currently trade at.

To explain my point, I made the table below. I compared Nu Holdings to two traditional banks closer to the products Nu offers — Bank of America Corporation (BAC) and Capital One Financial Corporation (COF), and also two digital banks, SoFi and Chime Financial, Inc. (CHYM).

Analyzing past and future growth of revenue and net income, Nu has better numbers in almost every case compared to all peers. Net income margin, ROE, and ROA are higher than those of all peers. I think this justifies a higher valuation than traditional banks and a valuation closer to digital banks based in the U.S..

Price to Sales and Price to Book are higher for Nubank, but in my view that mostly reflects the market's expectations for the company. The fact that Nubank will soon start doing business on the U.S. could also reduce the discount the stock has compared to peers in the U.S. That's why I think that, even my base case is very conservative and I would not be surprised if Nu trades close to mid 30x P/E by the end of 2026, which would translate to a price target between $25-$30 per share.

Based on the scenario analysis above, I see Nubank's risk/reward as favorable for investors. The asymmetry I see in the base and bull case makes the stock very attractive. The downside seems contained, since I don't see the company trading at traditional bank valuations, while the delivery of results in its expansion could easily increase the company's valuation.

Catalysts And The Future Of Nubank

The stock could react very positively if Nubank keeps executing well in Mexico and Colombia, because only 30% to 50% of the population in Latin America over the age of 15 has an account with a financial institution. This shows how much room there is for financial inclusion in the region, and Nubank already proved in Brazil that it can attract customers who were underserved by traditional banks.

The fact that many customers got their first credit card with Nubank in Brazil, and about 43% of them started saving money with the bank, shows that the company can attract new clients and deepen the relationship by offering more products over time.

Another important driver that could change how Nu Holdings is valued is establishing operations in the U.S. If successful, the company would gain access to a much larger and stronger economy, reducing concentration in Latin America and its risks, and bringing the valuation closer to its peers based in the U.S.. Also, the U.S. has large Latin American communities that could naturally connect with Nubank's products, especially if the company can offer a product that connects in both countries and makes it easier to manage money between the U.S. and the customers' home countries. I see the U.S. market expansion as a long-term strategic move that could bring significant upside.

The current position of the company also gives room to keep growing. Nu ended the period with a capital position of $8.9 billion and available funding of $38.8 billion, while its net credit portfolio stood at $19 billion. To me, this shows that the company has enough liquidity to keep expanding its business from a position of strength, which is especially important for a company operating in riskier markets.

Looking ahead, I think Nu's future is very promising. The company launched more than 100 new products and features, is still expanding in Colombia and Mexico, and is already thinking about markets like the U.S.. At the same time, it's investing in AI across different parts of the business, from underwriting to customer experience and cross-selling.

Risks

Nu Holdings operates in a very volatile environment in Brazil. Over the last five years, the country has gone through large changes in interest rates (from 2%-15%) and different political administrations, yet the company was still able to remain profitable and keep expanding. Nubank had high spreads when interest rates were high, but dealt with credit risk. Then, it faced the opposite scenario. I don't think Nubank is going to face a very different environment as it expands to Colombia and Mexico, but I believe the company is prepared for that.

Another risk that I see is that Nubank is using the same strategy in Mexico and Colombia that it used in Brazil to attract deposits, offering high-yield savings accounts. The company currently offers savings up to 120% of the Brazilian Interbank Deposit Rates, meaning a return close to 17.6%, but if Nubank can't convert these deposits into profitable loans, risk increases.

Also, Nubank has been planning to start doing business in the U.S. in 2027, but although I see this expansion as an impressive next step, we need to understand that the company is going to compete in a different environment than it is used to, with tough competition. Latin America has proven to be a great market for Nubank, which makes me wonder why not expand to other Latin American countries first?

One more risk is currency. Nubank operates in countries that have weaker currencies than the dollar. Foreign exchange can affect reported results and returns, even if the business does well in local currency. Political and regulatory changes can also affect the company, since historically these countries are more volatile than the U.S..

Nubank also faces competition from digital banks in both Colombia and Mexico. In Colombia, Nequi is probably the strongest competitor. The bank is owned by Bancolombia, giving a strong local presence and brand recognition, which has helped the bank reach almost 50% of the adult population already. Because in Colombia people still use cash, the structure Nequi has is a competitive advantage compared to Nubank. Lulobank is another player worth paying attention to, since it offers similar products like credit cards, high-yield savings accounts, and loans.

In Mexico, I think Klar is one of the most relevant competitors, especially after the acquisition of Bineo. Hey Banco is another digital bank that is growing in Mexico. Nubank is not entering an empty market like it did when it was the only digital bank facing traditional banks in Brazil.

Conclusion

I believe that Nu Holdings is a very interesting opportunity, as it is an attractive company that is growing consistently, and the stock currently trades at a reasonable price. The company has been showing better efficiency, growth in revenue, and expansion in Latin America and now the U.S.. As a client, I think that Nubank is going to be successful in its expansion in Colombia and Mexico. There are risks involved, mostly because the company operates in volatile countries, but at current levels, in my view, Nubank is a Buy.

data.title: American Express: Some Caution Into Earnings
data.description:
American Express (AXP) shares have been soft YTD. Earnings are next week on April 23 and the stock is looking to step up to regain some much-needed momentum. As geopolitical tensions have largely defused, the stock has bounced off the lows, up 8% this week alone. While I could paint a picture of why now is still an attractive entry point, there are several concerns I have about the upcoming earnings report that may pause the recent rally. With that being said, I think any dip could be short-lived and would be a buyer on weakness.

Overview and Setup Into Earnings

The company's total billed business can be broken down into three segments - consumer services, commercial services, and international. Quite simply, this is the domestic spending by consumers, businesses, and then both of those groups in an international setting. The more spending that happens in a given quarter, the better AXP performs.

Despite everything that's been happening in the last year - from tariffs to international conflicts - transaction growth across the business has been remarkably resilient. While new card growth in Q4 in the US was a little slower than the prior four-quarter trend (1.3 million vs. ~1.5 million trailing four-quarter average), they were still able to put up notably positive results.

Source: Investor Presentation

The issue that concerns me ahead of earnings is sequential spending developments. Given the international debacle that has been the Iran war and the DHS shutdown causing friction at US airports, let alone higher domestic and global gas prices, there could be pressure on all aspects of the business when American Express goes to report next week.

The international division had a 12% growth rate in Q4, much more than the 9% the US consumer services segment put up, as well as just 3% growth for the commercial services segment. So, this has been accretive to growth and a faltering in international could lead to pressure on share prices. Airline growth was already weak in Q4 and sequential trends undoubtedly got worse given geopolitics and DHS issues. Restaurant spending was also likely to deteriorate q/q in the US given higher gas prices (and the surge, effectively, from $3/gallon to $4/gallon in the US). Lodging will suffer as a derivative of both of those, so it's tough to see sequential growth remotely improving on the travel & entertainment side of the business.

Source: Investor Presentation

To be fair, T&E is only about 30% of the business. The vast majority of card spend comes from the Goods & Services segment; however, given that when costs hurt the consumer wallet, even if the company does have a higher-income consumer, spending declines. While we do not have March retail sales data, January and February on a core basis were solid, up 20bps m/m and 40bps m/m, respectively. Part of this is due to the easier January compared to the prior year period. March is an extremely tough lap as it is the pre-tariff pull-forward lap (retail sales rose 1% m/m last year in March and then fell off in April and May).

Source: Trading Economics

If retail spending domestically and abroad soured in March, not just because of the tough compare, then it's going to be difficult to get upside to revenue growth guidance in the near-term. This could pressure shares that have recently bounced.

It's also important to discuss their strategy with AI. Just two days ago, the company announced it would be buying Hyper, an agentic expense management company. This builds on their previous partnership established in 2024. While there were no terms of the deal, as it is presumably small and immaterial to the financial profile of American Express, this pushes American Express into the frontier of helping to make businesses more efficient from an expense management standpoint. This expands their reach vertically from just providing spending capabilities through commercial credit lines such that businesses can now leverage things like a mini FP&A analyst or a mini travel agent, all the while reducing human costs in call centers. Gaining a deeper relationship with their customers by having more touchpoints will enable them to learn more and expand even faster. To be clear, I wouldn't expect an acquisition like this to pay dividends any time soon given the small scale, but it is certainly an interesting step in the right direction.

Thinking Through Guidance and Forward Estimates

Over the last four fiscal years, the company has averaged an 11% revenue CAGR. 2025 was close to on par with that, as they reported a 10% revenue increase ex-interest expense and 9% on a constant currency basis. The company also put up 15% in adjusted EPS growth, once again contributing to its title as an earnings compounder.

When the company gave guidance back in late January, it provided the following:

  • Revenue growth 9-10%
  • Adj. EPS $17.30-$17.90

Within this guide, discount revenue, net card fees, and service fees are the primary contributors, of which net card fees have been growing the fastest. In fact, they've been growing at a 17% CAGR since Q4 of 2019. Growth remains steady in the mid-teens, albeit off of 2025 highs when they briefly reached 20%. The point should be taken, though, that fewer card fees are charged if there is less overall spending volume, which is my concern ahead of the upcoming report.

The way I think about AXP from here is current guidance plus a premium given their track record, as well as a kicker in the out year to compensate for potential first-half weakness, all the while using their long-term guidance as a helpful framework. Recall that their long-term aspiration is revenue growth of 10%+ and mid-teens EPS growth.

For 2026, the midpoint of guidance is $17.60. I'm looking for closer to $18 as their consistent history of earnings beats should support at least low-single-digit upside to this with a guidance raise about halfway through the year.

While the average beat over the last decade for AXP earnings is about 16%, that's inflated by low base effects. More accurately, the median EPS beat of nearly 3% is the right way to think about potential upside to current guidance on a full-year basis.

Source: Passage Research

While this is achievable, the issue is principally in Q1 the street is looking for about 9.5% revenue growth versus Q4 '25 revenue growth of about 10.5%. A one point sequential slowdown does not at all seem like enough of a buffer to compensate against the global macro situation that has transpired. A 3-4 point buffer from street estimates would seem more appropriate and I see a real risk that they do not meet this estimate. The natural caveat is that even if AXP trades down on the report because of what I've outlined, the geopolitical situation is quickly evolving to be much less tense, and spending should improve above-trend into Q2, especially given easy domestic compares in April & May because of tariff laps. Thus, any dip may be short-lived.

On the upcoming earnings call, I think it's important that investors get an update on the health of the US consumer. Last quarter they had spoken to very strong momentum, especially with higher-income consumers and didn't directly address the low or middle-income consumer. Instead, they chose to focus on the Platinum platform instead. While American Express skews higher income with their customer cohort, an update on all income levels of consumers in the US would be particularly insightful. Additionally, on Platinum Travel, I am looking for an update in travel bookings. They had noted on the last earnings call that they saw an uptick of 30% in travel bookings in 4Q because of their Platinum Travel app launch. Additionally, their partnership with Resy drove more engagement and led Platinum restaurant spending to be up 20%. It will be interesting to hear how those numbers evolved in Q1. If they stay remotely steady, that could be a sign of increased resilience from the higher-income consumer and a better forward outlook for American Express.

Balance Sheet & Valuation

The balance sheet remains in solid shape and AXP has a robust capital allocation program designed to reward shareholders. Investors can have confidence that dividends will continue to grow and share repurchases will be a consistent way to bring incremental value to the total return profile.

AXP has incredibly high returns on capital with the return on average equity staying above 32% for the last 6 years. Last year it was 34%. That more than supports the $5.3 billion in annual share purchases they conducted and $2.3 billion in dividends they shelled out. Against a CET1 ratio of 10.5% (the CET1 target is 10-11%) and a 58% increase in the dividend on a 3-year basis, the company remains incredibly well-capitalized and well-run. The dividend this year alone has already been slated for a 16% increase. The yield is now 1.2%, and while that may be low, mid-teens EPS growth should be enough to propel the stock to return double digits each year. While nothing is guaranteed, it's rare to have that type of compounding without stock appreciation.

The valuation is not particularly attractive here on an LTM EV/EBITDA basis as it trips the +1 standard deviation level versus its historical average. On a forward basis, it's trading just a couple of turns south of this, in the high-teens range. While it's a largely consistent earnings compounder, this is a small headwind going into earnings, especially given the moves this week.

Source: Passage Research

On a P/E basis, using our $18 figure and high-teens earnings growth next year (I am assuming low-single-digit outperformance versus their 2026 estimated base), we're looking at approximately $21.20 in EPS power next year relative to the street at $20.10. While nominally that's only 5% upside, the steady nature of this business actually makes for a compelling opportunity.

Risks

One of the primary risks with American Express is write-offs and delinquencies. The company tends to skew in the less risky bucket with both 30+ days past due receivables and net write-off rates, given that they cater to a higher-income consumer than credit card competitors. For instance, the 30+ days past due percentage of total loans and receivables has been constant the last five quarters at 1.3%. It's not to say that delinquencies can't tick up, they certainly can, but there are no present signs of it. The other risk with buying shares is that the consumer may be more permanently impacted by what's transpired in the first quarter of the year which would impact spending for the balance of AXP's fiscal year. While I see this as unlikely, we have to remember that on many gauges, consumer confidence is at a multi-decade low.

Conclusion

There is risk going into AXP earnings next week and street estimates do not seem to offer enough of a buffer. While Q1 could be a soft quarter for the company, I am looking to Q2 for a notable rebound, especially with easy comparisons. This should help to make any dip short-lived on the stock.

data.title: Our Top 10 High Growth Dividend Stocks - April 2026
data.description:
Note: The stocks shortlisted and highlighted in this article are not buy recommendations per se but rather candidates for further research. Before making any investments, please use due diligence, considering your personal goals and risk tolerance. Also, some sections in the article may be repetitive from month to month for the benefit of the new readers. The regular readers could skip them. However, we recommend that first-time readers refer to our BLOGPOST here to get a detailed explanation of our selection methodology/process and additional criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks.

Introduction:

Every month, we start with about 400 to 500 dividend-paying stocks and use our proprietary and unique filtering criteria to bring down the number of selections to a small subset of about 20 stocks. The emphasis here is on high growth rather than high yield. Generally, we end up with stocks that pay dividend yields in the range of 1% to 1.5%, which is in line with what the S&P500 pays as well. These 20 stocks are from diverse sectors and industries, but they all have one aspect in common. They appear to be in their period of hypergrowth with great momentum. As a final step, we analyze each of the 20 stocks and use our judgment to come up with a final list of just 10 stocks. The majority of our selections (if not all) have a market cap of $10 billion or more.

Who should follow this series?

The stocks highlighted in this series are for investors who are in their accumulation phase and are more interested in growing their capital at a rapid pace rather than in their current income. The focus of the selections is dividend growth and growth sustainability rather than high current yield. In that sense, the risk profile of these stocks is generally higher, and they are not suitable for conservative investors or investors who need high current incomes to sustain their lifestyle.

Market Outlook

Currently, the market is overwhelmingly driven by the events happening in the Iran war and the oil prices. Nothing else really seems to matter right now. That said, the market is learning to ignore the day-to-day noise coming from the war theatre and has pulled back from the brink in the last few days.

The war has obviously resulted in much higher energy prices, and they are likely to stay elevated for some time even if the war ends soon, which appears uncertain at this point. The higher energy prices will soon start showing up in consumer inflation. If the inflation is significantly higher in the coming months, the Fed's hands will be tied, and instead of cutting the interest rates, they will probably be talking of raising the rates. Though it is too early to say anything at this point, the odds for a rate cut in 2026 are quite bleak. In fact, the markets are predicting no rate cuts during 2026. You can see the current odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website. In any case, it may be best to keep some dry powder and buy opportunistically.

All that being said, we can never be sure of the future direction of the markets. We intend to stay invested in stocks that have the best chance of growing rapidly in a bull market and not losing as much in a down market. The purpose of this series of articles is to highlight an outstanding list of high-growth stocks that also happen to have positive momentum behind them.

Selection Criteria for High-Growth Dividend Stocks:

Note: For more details and background information on this series, please read our blog post (the link provided at the beginning of the article). This section is common among articles under this series. The regular readers could skip this section.

This section briefly describes our selection criteria and process. For detailed information, please refer to the blog post (link provided earlier). We define our basic selection criteria here to bring our target list to roughly 400 stocks. Based on these criteria, we also calculate a "Dividend Safety Quality Score." In brief, the Quality Score is derived using the following factors:

  • Current Yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth)
  • Payout ratio (dividend per share divided by EPS)
  • Past three years and five-year dividend growth
  • Chowder number - the sum of the 5-year dividend growth rate and the current yield
  • EPS growth (average of the previous five years of growth and expected next 3-5 years' growth)
  • Debt/capital and Debt/asset ratios
  • S&P's credit ratings (Standard & Poor's Global Ratings)
  • Distance from 52-week high (current price minus 52-week high price)
  • Sales or Revenue growth during the past five years.

Additional Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks

We will now use the following additional criteria to filter out stocks that would fit the mold of high-growth DGI stocks.

  • The payment ratio (on a cash-flow basis or EPS basis) is less than 95% but preferably less than 80%.
  • 5-year annualized dividend growth (or 3-year growth rate) is at least 7.5% or greater. However, some exceptions can be made.
  • Chowder number (5-year dividend growth rate plus the current dividend yield) >= 9.

Until this stage of filtering, we kept the base criteria loose enough to allow a wide variety of stocks to make the list. However, we will perform additional filtering in the next stage to get the best candidates.

Most growth investors appreciate how important the earnings growth (earnings per share - EPS) is for a stock to grow its dividend rapidly. Without growth in earnings, the company cannot grow its dividends sustainably for long. So, our focus ought to be on earnings growth.

In our spreadsheet, we will add four more columns of data for each of the stocks:

  • EPS (earnings per share) Quality Rating
  • Last Qtr EPS change % % (actual)
  • Current Qtr EPS change % (est.)
  • Current year EPS change % (est.).

We will now assign weights to these four sets of data for each stock and add them to the original "Dividend Safety Quality Score" to come up with a modified Quality Score tilted in favor of high dividend growth stocks. We will call this column "High-Growth Quality Score".

Narrowing Down the List To 20 Stocks:

The criteria listed above provide us with a list of roughly 350-400 stocks to work with. We will now filter it down and select roughly 50 stocks based on the following methodology.

  • We first select the top 20 stocks based on the highest High Growth-Quality-Score (adjusted for sector or industry over concentration). Please see Table-1A below.
  • We then take the top 10 stocks based on the highest 5-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1B below.
  • We will also select the top 10 stocks based on the highest 3-year dividend growth. This is to account for more recent growth. Please see Table-1C below.
  • Finally, we will select the top 10 stocks based on the average of EPS Rating (earnings per share) and RS rating (Relative Strength). (EPS and RS ratings are taken from IBD; subscription required). Please see Table-1D below.

Table-1A: Top 20 Highest HG-Quality-Score Stocks

Table-1B: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (5 years)

Table-1C: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (3 years)

Table-1D: Top 10 Stocks with the Highest EPS and RS Rating

We will now combine these 50 stocks from the above four tables and remove any duplicates from this combined list, as some stocks may have qualified based on multiple criteria.

The following stocks appeared two times: AU, COKE, CVE, FIX, TRGP, and WWD (6 duplicates).

Appeared four times: VRT (3 duplicates).

We are now left with 41 (50 - 9) names.

We will also remove any names where the revenue growth (over the past five years) has been negative. There is one on the list. We remove GE. There are also three others that don't have the sales growth figures readily available, but we will give them the benefit of the doubt and keep them for now. Now, we have 40 names left.

We will now calculate the average of EPS and RS (relative strength) ratings and remove any names that have an average of less than 60. This removes WIT, BAM, AEG, GGAL, TKO, SYK, PGR, MSFT, and V. We now have 31 (40-9) names.

We will also remove any names with both the EPS and RS less than 80. We have no such case. We still have 31 names.

Next, we will remove any stock that has an RS rating of less than 60. There are none in our list. We are now left with 30 names.

Finally, we will need to ensure that there are not too many stocks from the same industry segment (or even a sector). In cases where there is overconcentration, we will keep the best ones that also have some momentum and tailwind. During this process, we prioritize the stocks that have higher Relative Strength and EPS ratings.

Finance-Bank-major:

Finance-Bank-Foreign: We keep HSBC and BBVA, but remove NWG.

Finance-Regional Banks: We remove BK.

Financial Services and Misc: We remove IBKR and CBOE.

In all, we removed eleven names at this stage, so we are finally left with 20 (31-11) names, which are presented in the table below (in order of the sector/segment):

(WWD), (FIX), (HSBC), (BBVA), (SNX), (CCJ), (TPR), (AGX), (AXIA), (VRT), (CASY), (AU), (CB), (KGC), (CVE), (EC), (LRCX), (COKE), (TS), and (WCC).

Table 2: Top 20 High-Growth DGI Stocks of the Month:

Final Step: Selection of Top 10 High-Growth DGI Stocks

Until now, we have mostly used our rule-based filtering criteria to come to a list of 20 stocks. However, we carefully avoided the high concentration of any one sector or industry segment. Now, this final step of bringing down the number of selections to just ten stocks is mostly a subjective process based on our judgment. We try to keep the group as diverse as possible, representing many sectors and industry segments. We encourage readers to look at our wider selection of 20 stocks and see if they come up with anything different and appropriate for their goals. Nonetheless, we describe below how we go about selecting these ten stocks for the month.

We sort our list of 20 stocks by sector and industry segment, then in decreasing order of high-growth quality score. We try to select one stock from each industry segment from top to bottom; however, at times, multiple stocks could appear from the same sector. We will try to keep the best stock in each industry segment based on multiple factors, such as the quality score and the recent momentum. We also need to recognize that this is 'not' a permanent buy-and-hold portfolio, and some stocks get reshuffled every month, so the focus is on selecting stocks that are likely to do well on a short- to medium-term basis. That's why we are also going to give priority to stocks with solid EPS ratings and high positive momentum (Relative strength).

We want to caution that valuation is not a factor in the selection of these stocks, so some of these may be highly overvalued. Due to high valuation, their yields tend to be on the lower side as well, in the range of 1% to 2%.

Here are our top 10 selections for this month.

Current Month Selections:

(WWD), (FIX), (SNX), (CCJ), (AGX), (VRT), (AU), (CVE), (LRCX), and (TS).

Previous Month Selections:

(WWD), (FIX), (CCJ), (TPR), (VRT), (AU), (CVE), (LRCX), (COKE), and (TS).

Since our methodology is largely based on a filtering process, we would like to point out that once a stock has appeared on our list, it may repeat for at least a couple of months. On average, we see about 30% to 40% turnover from one month to another, but it can vary greatly. This month, the turnover is quite low as only two (out of ten) stocks are being replaced.

If a stock from the previous month has been dropped or replaced, it does not mean that the stock is no longer attractive. It simply means that our filtering process has found something more appealing in terms of momentum if two stocks are at par qualitatively.

Table 3: (High Growth DGI)

Performance

We had a pause in growth during the Feb-Mar period after many months of impressive gains. However, this past month, our portfolio returned to growth and posted impressive double-digit gains in spite of a difficult market due to geopolitical fallout. In fact, the performance outpaced the S&P500 by over 8 percentage points.
